002_02e9ae5f.question: How many hours per fortnight can a Student visa holder work while their course is in session?
002_02e9ae5f.ground_truth: Up to 48 hours a fortnight while the course of study or training is in session. Students studying a master's degree by research or a doctoral degree, and their families, have no work-hour limit.
002_02e9ae5f.answer: Student visa holders can work up to 48 hours a fortnight while the course is in session [C1]. However, if the Student is studying a master’s degree by research or doctoral degree, they and their family members can work more than 48 hours a fortnight [C1][C2]. Additionally, if the student is studying a master’s degree by coursework, or a master’s degree (Extended), their family members can also work more than 48 hours a fortnight, but the student can still only work up to 48 hours a fortnight while their course is in session [C1][C2].

003_d3219916.question: What health insurance must Student visa holders and their family members maintain?
003_d3219916.ground_truth: They must have and maintain Overseas Student Health Cover (OSHC) from an approved Australian provider for the whole stay, unless an exception applies (e.g. Norwegian students under the Norwegian National Insurance Scheme, Swedish students under Kammarkollegiet, or Belgian students under the Reciprocal Health Care Agreement).
003_d3219916.answer: Student visa holders and their family members must maintain Overseas Student Health Cover (OSHC) [C1][C2][C3]. This OSHC must be from an approved Australian health insurance provider, unless an 'Exception' applies [C2][C3]. They must maintain their OSHC until they leave Australia [C3].

005_e040722e.question: How much money must a student show for 12 months of living costs when applying for this visa, and what is the amount for an accompanying partner or child?
005_e040722e.ground_truth: The declared 12-month living costs are AUD29,710 for the student, AUD10,394 for an accompanying partner, and AUD4,449 for an accompanying child.
005_e040722e.answer: A student must show AUD29,710 for 12 months of living costs [C1]. For an accompanying partner, the amount is AUD10,394 [C1], and for a child, it is AUD4,449 [C1].

Course fees
Use the first 12 months of your course fees. If your course is 12 months or less, use the total cost.
Deduct any costs you have already paid –you must provide evidence you have paid them, such as a

If you are in Australia and your course has already started, calculate the course fee for 12 months
starting on the date you lodge your application.
Example 1: your course fee is AUD50,000 for 3 years. The fee for 12 months is the total course cost
divided by the number of years you will be studying. AUD50,000 divided by 3 years = AUD16,666, less
any amount you have already paid if relevant.
Example 2: your course fee is AUD15,000 for 10 months and you have already paid AUD5,000.
Deduct the amount you have already paid from the total amount. AUD15,000 - AUD5,000 =
AUD10,000.

009_617a89bf.question: Who is eligible to be nominated as a guardian for a Student visa applicant under 18?
009_617a89bf.ground_truth: A nominated guardian must be the student's parent, a person who has custody of the student, or a relative aged 21 or older who is nominated in writing by a parent or person with custody.
009_617a89bf.answer: A nominated guardian can be a relative of the student, aged 21 years or older, nominated in writing by a parent or person who has custody of the student [C3][C4]. This relative can be a parent or step-parent, grandparent or step-grandparent, sibling or step-sibling, or aunt, uncle, step-aunt or step-uncle [C3][C4]. A nominated guardian must also be the parent of the Student visa holder or a person who has custody of the student [C5]. Additionally, a nominated guardian can have Australian citizenship, a permanent visa, a substantive temporary visa for the duration of the student's stay or until they turn 18, or have applied for or hold a subclass 590 visa [C3][C4].

014_bdf26786.question: What is the difference between a Bridging visa A and a Bridging visa B for a Student visa applicant?
014_bdf26786.ground_truth: A Bridging visa A (BVA) starts automatically if the applicant's substantive visa expires while their new application is being processed, but it does not allow travel and cancels on departure. A Bridging visa B (BVB) can be granted for substantial travel reasons and allows the holder to return to Australia while their application is being processed.
014_bdf26786.answer: A Bridging visa A (BVA) does not allow the holder to travel outside Australia, as it cancels on departure [C2][C3]. In contrast, a Bridging visa B (BVB) can be considered if the holder has a BVA and substantial reasons for travel [C1][C3]. This implies that a BVB may allow the holder to travel outside Australia and return, whereas a BVA does not [C1]. If you have a BVA and leave Australia, it will cease [C1].

019_5832e547.question: What is the minimum IELTS Academic score required for a Student visa for tests taken on or after 7 August 2025?
019_5832e547.ground_truth: A minimum overall IELTS Academic score of 6.0 is required, reduced to 5.5 if the principal course is packaged with at least 10 weeks of ELICOS (or an eligible Foundation/Pathway program), or 5.0 if packaged with at least 20 weeks of ELICOS.
019_5832e547.answer: The minimum IELTS Academic score required for a Student visa for tests taken on or after 7 August 2025 is 6.0 [C2]. However, if the principal course is accompanied with either at least 10 weeks of ELICOS or a Foundation program, the minimum score is 5.5 [C2], and if accompanied by at least 20 weeks of ELICOS, the minimum score is 5.0 [C2].

021_5fec7034.question: Which Student visa applicants are exempt from paying the visa application charge (VAC)?

Student and family members supported by the Department of Defence [C1][C2], 
Secondary exchange students [C1][C2][C4], 
Students affected by education provider default and lodge a new Student visa application within 12 months of the provider default day [C2][C4], 
Family members of students enrolled in a postgraduate research course [C2][C4], 
Students affected by COVID-19 [C2][C4].

024_5e1e0f25.question: What three clusters of GenAI perception did the factor analysis identify in the GenAI research-practices survey?
024_5e1e0f25.ground_truth: The factor analysis identified three clusters: "GenAI as a work horse," "GenAI as a language assistant only," and "GenAI as a research accelerator."
024_5e1e0f25.answer: The factor analysis identified three clusters of GenAI perception: "GenAI as a work horse", "GenAI as a language assistant only", and "GenAI as a research accelerator" [C1][C2]. These clusters represent different types of integrity assessments of GenAI use in research [C3]. The "GenAI as a work horse" cluster includes researchers who consider using GenAI for tasks such as creating and editing software codes, supporting statistical analysis, and recognizing patterns in data [C3]. The "GenAI as a research accelerator" cluster includes researchers who are generally very positive about using GenAI in almost all use cases, particularly in relation to data analysis and research design [C5].

030_6cb06e2e.question: Did the study find a difference in GenAI use or research-integrity assessment between men and women?
030_6cb06e2e.ground_truth: No, the study found no significant differences in either GenAI use or research-integrity assessment between men and women.
030_6cb06e2e.answer: The study did not find any significant differences in GenAI use or research-integrity assessment between men and women [C1][C3][C4]. Junior researchers used GenAI more than senior colleagues, while no significant gender differences were observed [C4].

031_2dd86cd6.question: How did GenAI usage differ between junior and senior researchers?

However, in terms of research integrity assessment, no substantial differences between respondents from different academic ages were observed [C2].

032_00c481d5.question: Which GenAI tool did most survey respondents have in mind when answering the survey, and what other tools were mentioned?
032_00c481d5.ground_truth: Most respondents were thinking of ChatGPT (n = 1,550). Copilot (Microsoft and GitHub combined) was second (n = 176), followed by Grammarly (n = 101), Google's Bard (n = 76), Dall-E (n = 74), and DeepL (n = 69).
032_00c481d5.answer: Most survey respondents had ChatGPT in mind when answering the survey, with 1550 respondents indicating this [C1]. Other tools mentioned include Copilot, which was the second most mentioned tool with 176 respondents [C1], and Grammarly, which was mentioned by 101 respondents [C1]. Additionally, Google's Bard was also mentioned [C1]. Some respondents had more general tools in mind, like Grammarly, while others had highly specialized tools in mind [C3].

034_116b8241.question: How did humanities researchers compare to other fields in GenAI use and assessment?
034_116b8241.ground_truth: Humanities scholars reported using GenAI tools least frequently of all main research areas and gave the least positive research-integrity assessments; 19.3% of humanities respondents gave an overall assessment of 3 or less on the 7-point scale.
034_116b8241.answer: In cluster 1 – “GenAI as a work horse” – researchers from the humanities, who work on data produced by themselves (26.4 %), are found alongside clinical medical researchers (30.5 %) and theoretical natural scientists (31.7 %) [C1]. Usage of GenAI differed by main research area, with technical and quantitative sciences reporting slightly higher usage and more positive assessments [C2]. However, the comparison of humanities researchers to other fields in GenAI use and assessment in terms of specifics beyond cluster 1 and general usage patterns is not detailed [C1][C2].

question remain about not only whether, but how academics might use 
GenAI in research and how they assess its research integrity implications 
across various use cases. Indeed, research practices as diverse as plan­
ning experiments, writing project proposals, generating and collecting 
data, analysing it, reporting it or transforming it into lay-accessible 
content can all potentially be assisted or conducted by GenAI tools.

However, whether researchers actually do this and how they assess 
research integrity aspects of using GenAI for these various cases, remains 
largely unknown.
